---
bibliography: ../references.bib
---

# Introduction

This document reports the `r assessment_name` for `r species_label`
(*`r species_scientific`*) in the `r assessment_area`. Bigeye tuna stock
assessments in the WCPO have been conducted regularly since the late 1990s
[@langley_stock_2008; @harley_stock_2009; @harley_stock_2010;
@davies_stock_2011; @harley_stock_2014; @mckechnie_stock_2017;
@vincent_incorporation_2018; @ducharme-barth_stock_2020]. As in previous
assessments, the objectives are to estimate population quantities that indicate
stock status and fishing impacts, including recruitment, biomass, biomass
depletion, fishing mortality, and management reference points.

The assessment methodology follows the integrated modelling approach
[@fournier_general-theory_1982] implemented in MULTIFAN-CL (MFCL)
[@fournier_multifan-cl_1998; @hampton_spatially-disaggregated_2001;
@kleiber_multifan-cl_2019]. MFCL implements a size-based, age- and
spatially-structured population model. Model parameters are estimated by
maximising an objective function that combines likelihood components for the
observed data with penalty terms used to constrain selected biological or
fishery processes.

Each new assessment normally updates catch, effort, size-composition, tagging,
biological, and abundance-index inputs; incorporates new software or analysis
methods; and revisits assumptions that may affect stock-status estimates.
Advice from the WCPFC Scientific Committee, pre-assessment workshops, peer
review, and supporting analysis papers guides this process. The
`r assessment_year` report should identify the final data period, model
structure, software version, and model-development sequence used for the
accepted assessment, and should separate confirmed results from material that
remains under review.

## Study Objectives

The objectives of this report are to:

- describe the final assessment data and model configuration;
- document the main model development steps and sensitivity analyses;
- present diagnostics and fits to the data sources used in the model;
- summarise structural and estimation uncertainty for management quantities;
- provide stock-status advice from the agreed model set.

## Background

The assessment uses a set of models to explore structural uncertainty. Such a
grid is designed to represent interactions among selected axes of uncertainty
related to biological assumptions, data inputs, data treatment, and model
structure. The axes are usually selected from one-off sensitivities around a
diagnostic or base model. Variation in the key management quantities across the
grid should be considered carefully when formulating management advice.

The `r assessment_year` report should identify the diagnostic or base model,
the one-off sensitivities used to screen candidate assumptions, and the final
structural-uncertainty axes used for management advice. Where estimation
uncertainty is combined with structural uncertainty, the report should describe
how uncertainty intervals and stock-status probabilities are calculated.

> **TODO:** Add the final supporting papers, PAW/SC guidance, peer-review
> context, and confirmed uncertainty axes that directly apply to the
> `r assessment_year` assessment.

## Stock Structure

Bigeye tuna are distributed throughout tropical and subtropical waters of the
Pacific, Indian and Atlantic Oceans. Genetic studies indicate limited mixing
between Atlantic bigeye tuna and bigeye tuna in the Indo-Pacific regions
[@chow_genetic_2000; @gonzalez_genetic_2008; @grewe_population_2019], but there
is currently no clear evidence for genetic population structure within the
Pacific Ocean [@grewe_assessment_1998; @moore_defining_2020;
@natasha_no_2022]. Conventional and electronic tagging data are broadly
consistent with a continuous Pacific distribution with occasional long-distance
movement, while also indicating important spatial heterogeneity in movement,
growth, recruitment, and fishery processes [@schaefer_movements_2015;
@moore_defining_2020].

Recent reviews of population structure and spatial stratification
[@hamer_review_2023] suggested that bigeye tuna are likely one genetic stock
in the Pacific,
while also noting that the western and eastern Pacific, tropical and temperate
areas, and the Indonesia/Philippines/Vietnam region are unlikely to be fully
mixed for assessment purposes. The current assessment should document whether
the spatial stratification remains unchanged or whether an alternative regional
structure is adopted.

The final report should state the exact assessment area, eastern boundary,
regional stratification, and rationale for any regions used to represent
tagging information, archipelagic areas, or other spatial heterogeneity. Maps
and regional summaries should match the accepted model input files.

> **TODO:** Confirm the `r assessment_year` regional structure, the number of
> model regions, stock-structure evidence, movement assumptions, and changes
> from the most recent assessment.

## Biological Characteristics

Bigeye tuna are moderately fast-growing tuna and may reach fork lengths of
approximately 200 cm [@aires-da-silva_improved_2015; @farley_age_2017].
Growth parameters are influential biological inputs to the assessment
[@mckechnie_stock_2017; @vincent_incorporation_2018], and recent assessments
have considered otolith, tag-increment, and conditional age-at-length
information [@eveson_integrated_2020; @farley_age_2020].

Bigeye tuna in the WCPO become reproductively active from about 80 cm fork
length, with most fish mature by approximately 120 cm fork length
[@farley_age_2017]. The assessment uses spawning potential rather than simple
spawning biomass because reproductive output depends on maturity, female
sex-ratio, and fecundity. Natural mortality is size- or age-dependent and is an
important source of biological uncertainty [@hampton_natural_2000;
@farley_age_2020].

The final report should document whether growth is fixed externally or
estimated within the assessment model, how conditional age-at-length data are
used, and how natural mortality is specified or estimated. These biological
settings affect growth, maturity-at-age translation, fishing mortality-at-age,
yield analyses, and the interpretation of depletion.

> **TODO:** Replace or refine this text with confirmed `r assessment_year`
> biological inputs, including growth, maturity, natural mortality,
> length-weight conversion, recruitment assumptions, and conditional
> age-at-length data.

## Fisheries

Bigeye tuna are an important component of tuna fisheries throughout the Pacific
Ocean. Larger bigeye tuna are targeted by longline fleets, particularly in
tropical waters of the WCPO, while smaller fish are caught incidentally by purse
seine vessels, especially in sets associated with floating objects. Bigeye tuna
are a principal target species in tropical longline fisheries supplying frozen
and fresh sashimi markets.

Collaborative work among SPC, WCPFC, national fisheries agencies, and research
partners has improved catch statistics and fishery definitions for several
fleets, including Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam. The final
`r assessment_year` report should present the model fishery definitions, catch
history, spatial distribution of catch, and data coverage used in the accepted
input files.

The `r assessment_year` report should state the final number of fisheries,
distinguish extraction and index fisheries where applicable, and identify any
changes to gear, fleet, region, selectivity, or reporting-rate groupings. It
should also describe whether recent catch, effort, observer, or port-sampling
updates alter interpretation of the fishery time series.

> **TODO:** Update from the final `r assessment_year` fishery definitions,
> catch summaries, regional maps, and data-coverage diagnostics.
